coredata.dc:title: A facile synthetic strategy to simultaneously achieve ultra-wide PL redshift of carbon nanodots and their high selectivity and sensitivity for Mn2+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Wanrong
Li, Jiurong
Gong, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents a straightforward synthesis strategy for N, S co-doped carbon nanodots (N, S-CDs), aiming to achieve CDs emitting ultra-wide redshifts while obtaining excellent metal ion detection performance. The N, S-CDs display excellent fluorescence (FL) emission independence regardless of the excitation wavelength or concentration. By introducing a single dopant thiourea, the FL emission of CDs was red-shifted from 445 to 575 nm (∼130 nm). The doped N and S elements are discovered to cause the introduction of defective energy levels and an elevation in oxidation on the surface of CDs, which could potentially be the crucial factor for the ultra-wide redshift of FL emission. Also, it is investigated that solvent engineering is essential for the redshift of the emission peak, which can also be an effective way to synthesize multicolor CDs. Furthermore, the N, S-CDs can be utilized for measuring the water content in methanol solvent based on their solvent effects. Additionally, N, S-CDs have the capability of detecting Mn2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. The interaction of CDs with Mn2+ resulted in an obvious change in both the UV absorption spectrum and FL lifetime. To put it differently, the reaction mechanism between CDs and Mn2+ probably includes mixed processes of dynamic and static quenching. Zeta potential tests additionally indicate that the FL quenching is strongly correlated with the surface condition of CDs. The outcomes and discoveries offer a fresh and viable method for achieving the simultaneous production of long-wavelength glowing CDs and their analysis and detection of metal ions as fluorescent probes.

head.ce:title: A facile synthetic strategy to simultaneously achieve ultra-wide PL redshift of carbon nanodots and their high selectivity and sensitivity for Mn2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents a straightforward synthesis strategy for N, S co-doped carbon nanodots (N, S-CDs), aiming to achieve CDs emitting ultra-wide redshifts while obtaining excellent metal ion detection performance. The N, S-CDs display excellent fluorescence (FL) emission independence regardless of the excitation wavelength or concentration. By introducing a single dopant thiourea, the FL emission of CDs was red-shifted from 445 to 575 nm (∼130 nm). The doped N and S elements are discovered to cause the introduction of defective energy levels and an elevation in oxidation on the surface of CDs, which could potentially be the crucial factor for the ultra-wide redshift of FL emission. Also, it is investigated that solvent engineering is essential for the redshift of the emission peak, which can also be an effective way to synthesize multicolor CDs. Furthermore, the N, S-CDs can be utilized for measuring the water content in methanol solvent based on their solvent effects. Additionally, N, S-CDs have the capability of detecting Mn2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. The interaction of CDs with Mn2+ resulted in an obvious change in both the UV absorption spectrum and FL lifetime. To put it differently, the reaction mechanism between CDs and Mn2+ probably includes mixed processes of dynamic and static quenching. Zeta potential tests additionally indicate that the FL quenching is strongly correlated with the surface condition of CDs. The outcomes and discoveries offer a fresh and viable method for achieving the simultaneous production of long-wavelength glowing CDs and their analysis and detection of metal ions as fluorescent probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Excess Mn2+ in drinking water can lead to loss of appetite, vomiting, diarrhea, and gastrointestinal disorders [1,2]. In addition, Mn2+ in the human body can endanger the heart as well as the central nervous system and can even affect intelligence and reproductive function [3–5]. Therefore, proper detection of Mn2+ is needed to avoid ions overload and further health damage. In recent years, various analytical tools have been developed to detect Mn2+ and other different metal ions in water to reduce their fatal effects [6–9]. Carbon nanodots (CDs) are novel luminescent carbon nanomaterials that possess sizes among 0–10 nm and quasi-spherical morphologies. Their affordability, non-toxicity, excellent photoluminescence (PL), good stability, and good biocompatibility have garnered significant interest [10–12]. Additionally, CDs have numerous surface functional groups that include hydroxyl (-OH), amino (-NH2), and carboxyl (-COOH) groups, which enable their utilization as fluorescent nanoprobe for detection and analysis [13–16]. For the past few years, CDs as photoluminescent sensors for metal ions detection have shown the advantages of easy and fast operation.

Nowadays, CDs-based detection methods have been extended to multifunctional detection and different ions (metal/non-metal), but there are still some ions that have not received much attention, such as Mn2+ [17]. Additionally, these CDs-based photoluminescent probes still face many problems, such as complex synthesis processes, short emission wavelength, and high detection limits [8,9,17]. Currently, most of the reports focus on discovering individual problem solutions or using more complex methods to improve these drawbacks. Furthermore, there is a limited number of reports regarding the utilization of CDs fluorescent probes for Mn2+ detection. Table S1 lists the relevant literature reports, and it can be seen that most of the reports have the above problems, such as the complexity of the construction process of the fluorescent probes, which often need to be compounded with other materials; the emission wavelengths are concentrated in the blue-green emission band, and the short-wave emission restricts the detection environment; and the detection limit is higher, which cannot be applied to certain demanding operations, etc. Hence, exploring a fluorescent probe based on CDs with an uncomplicated synthesis procedure, extended wavelengths, and a rapid and highly responsive detection capability for Mn2+ ions is imperative.

In other words, as with other similar nanomaterials, the preparation for CDs will become particularly important, which will greatly affect their properties and applications [18,19]. Among the many preparation methods, the doping strategy using heteroatoms (N, S, B, P, etc.) in CDs is one of the facile ways to achieve high detection sensitivity and good selectivity [20]. Meanwhile, the most effective and fast method of achieving long-wavelength fluorescent-emitting CDs is to cause red-shifting of the emission peaks of short-wavelength CDs by heteroatom doping [17,18,21–23]. Based on these, heteroatom doping is expected to be a reasonable method to solve all of these drawbacks simultaneously.

The process of introducing heteroatoms can be categorized into single-element doping and multi-element doping. The modulation of the intrinsic properties of CDs has been demonstrated to have great potential through single-element doping, including the improvement of interactions between CDs and ions to be detected [6,8,24,25]. However, there has been a growing interest in multi-element doping in CDs for the synergistic effect between different heteroatoms. This effect can lead to the creation of unique electronic structures and a more significant enhancement of the CDs’ optical properties. Jiang et al. [26] discovered that co-doping with F and N can achieve the photoactivated FL enhancement of CDs. Liu et al. [27] found that the adsorption of O2 molecules on CDs during the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) process was promoted by co-doping with N and B. Li et al. [28] proposed that N, S-CDs were used as deposition modulating additives in the electrolyte to guide the uniform deposition of lithium ions. However, most of the reported polyatomic doping methods employ multiple dopants for the synthesis of CDs, which often leads to more impurities in the synthesized CDs, a complicated purification process, and a certain degree of raw material waste [29–32]. Thus, polyatomic doping via a single dopant on the basis of a suitable precursor, with fewer feedstocks not only reduces the introduction of excessive impurities to a certain extent but also is a feasible method to optimize the synthesis of CDs with long-wavelength FL for ion detection [32].

In this work, 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene (2,7-DHN) served as the carbon source. While thiourea was used as a sole dopant carrier for introducing N and S dopants into the CDs. Different techniques were used to characterize the optical and structural characteristics of the CDs. By altering the reaction solvent, the N, S-CDs exhibited remarkable sensitivity to solvents, facilitating the production of solvent-regulated multicolor CDs. Furthermore, the co-doped CDs containing N and S elements exhibited the ability to measure the moisture content in methanol solution by their solvent-chromogenic characteristic. Moreover, these techniques were utilized for identifying metal ions present in water. The findings indicated that the co-doping strategy of introducing N and S elements greatly alters the surface state of the CDs as well as their chelating effect on Mn2+, resulting in good solubility in aqueous solutions and can function as a precise and responsive sensing system for detecting Mn2+ with high selectivity through the alterations in FL it induces. The FL intensity (∼575 nm) gradually decreased as the Mn2+ concentration increased, demonstrating remarkable sensitivity.

2,7-Dihydroxynaphthalene (2,7-DHN), thiourea, and all the salts, such as sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), aluminum chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3·6H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2H2O), chromic chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3·6H2O), manganese (II) chloride (MnCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl3), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), zirconium chloride (ZrCl4), silver nitrate (AgNO3), barium chloride (BaCl2·2H2O) and mercury dichloride (HgCl2) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd., China. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), formamide, ethylene glycol (EG), acetone, chloroform (Chl), toluene (Tol), and cyclohexane (Cyc) were from Sinopharm Chemistry Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Deionized water was from the laboratory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped CDs and undoped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 2,7-DHN (0.25 mmol) and thiourea (6 mmol) were dissolved in methanol (10 mL) and sonicated for 10 min. Once the reactants were fully dissolved, the solution was moved to the Teflon-lined autoclave with a capacity of 25 mL. Subsequently, it was subjected to heating in an oven under certain conditions (180 °C, 6 h). Finally, the solution was allowed to cool down gradually until it reached room temperature. The initial solution underwent two rounds of filtration using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Subsequently, it was subjected to 24 h of dialysis employing a dialysis bag (1000 Da, obtained from Sigma-Aldrich) to eliminate any remaining unreacted substances and impurities. The filtrate fraction was collected for further characterization.
The procedure for synthesizing undoped CDs was identical to that of the doped CDs, with the exception that no source for doping was included.

To analyze the size and morphology of the CDs, a JEM-2100F TEM (JEOL, Japan) was utilized to capture transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images. For the analysis of CDs' lattice structure, the x-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded using a D8 advanced diffractometer (Bruker, Germany; Kα = 1.5406 Å). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Nicolet 6700, USA) and Raman spectroscopy (LABHR-UV) were utilized for the analysis of the composition and structure of CDs. The x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (ESCALAB 250Xi, USA) was utilized to conduct elemental and compositional analyses. The UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV2700, Japan) was used to record the absorption spectrum of CDs. Using a time-resolved FL spectrometer (FL3-22, USA), the solution recorded the PL spectrum of CDs with varying metal ion detection and solvents at room temperature. An automatic delay stage (M-ILS300LM, USA) was used to achieve the delay in FL. The changes in the Zeta potential of CDs were measured using the Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern Corporation, UK).

To examine the functionality of solvent engineering for CDs during the doping process, the methanol used in the solvothermal process was replaced with formamide, acetone, chloroform, and ethanol, respectively, while keeping the rest of the conditions unchanged. The solvent-controlled multicolor CDs were synthesized in this way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in methanol
ce:section.ce:para: Solvent selectivity test: 10 μL N, S co-doped CDs solution was mixed with 10 mL of solvents with different polarities (DMSO, DMF, methanol, ethanol, formamide, water, ethylene glycol, acetone, chloroform, toluene, and cyclohexane). After homogenizing the solutions for 10 min, they were transferred into a colorimeter. Then the PL spectrum of the solutions was recorded at the excitation wavelength (λex) of 395 nm.

The sensitivity of detection for water content in methanol: A mixture of methanol with various water content was prepared. Then 10 μL N, S co-doped CDs solution was added to 10 mL of the prepared mixture and homogenized for 10 min. Then the PL spectrum of the mixed solutions was recorded (λex = 395 nm). By data analysis, the relationship between the FL intensity of the mixed solutions and the water content was obtained.

The selectivity of detection for metal ions: To measure the specificity of metal ion detection, various metal salts were dissolved in water to create standard ionic solutions. These solutions contained different metal ions such as Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Zr4+, Ag+, Ba2+ and Hg2+ at concentrations of 500 μM. Next, 0.1 mL of a solution containing N, S co-doped CDs was introduced into 5 mL of the pre-made standard ionic solutions. The mixture was thoroughly blended for a duration of 10 min. Then the PL spectrum of various solutions was measured while being excited at a wavelength of 395 nm.

The sensitivity of detection for Mn
2+
: Various quantities of MnCl2 were dissolved in water to acquire diverse concentrations of Mn2+ solutions, showcasing the sensitivity of detection. Next, 0.1 mL of a solution containing co-doped CDs was introduced into 5 mL of the previously prepared solutions. The mixture was thoroughly blended for a duration of 10 min. The PL spectrum of the above-combined solutions was recorded while being excited at a wavelength of 395 nm. By analyzing and fitting the data, a quasi-functional correlation was established between the FL intensity of the combined solutions and the concentrations of Mn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine the optimal process synthesis parameters for obtaining CDs with excellent optical properties, variable probing experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. S1, the length of hydrothermal time and the amount of thiourea doping can greatly affect the emission wavelength and luminous intensity of CDs, so choosing the appropriate hydrothermal time and the amount of thiourea doping is crucial for CDs. Fig. 1 a shows the orange-emitting N, S-CDs obtained by heating under optimal conditions (180 °C, 6 h) with 2,7-DHN (C source) and a single dopant thiourea (N, S source). Throughout the synthesis process, the FL emission underwent a red shift from blue to orange (∼130 nm), as depicted in Fig. 1b and 1c. The TEM image (Fig. 1d) shows that the CDs with doping have a good dispersion and a uniform quasi-spherical morphology with an average size of 5.68 nm (Fig. 1e). The HRTEM image of the N, S co-doped CDs (inset of Fig. 1d) showed relatively distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [33].

The XRD pattern (Fig. S2a) shows that both before and after doping, the CDs have a wide diffraction peak centered around 24°, which corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon probably attributed to highly disordered carbon [34,35]. In Fig. S2b, the Raman data exhibits two distinct peaks at 1360 and 1580 cm−1 of the CDs before and after doping, indicating that the CDs both possess graphite-like structures, consistent with the TEM images and XRD analysis findings [36].The D-peak at 1360 cm−1 and the G-peak at 1580 cm−1 can be respectively attributed to surface defects or the vibration of disordered sp3 hybridized carbon atoms, and the graphitized structures or the vibration of ordered sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [37]. The ID/IG intensity ratios of CDs before and following doping are 0.91 and 1.02, respectively, suggesting a decrease in graphitization and an increase in surface defects after doping. The increase in N and S-related surface functional groups may arouse the extensive redshift in FL emission [38–40]. The surface chemical groups of doped and undoped CDs were detected by FT-IR spectrum. Fig. S2c illustrates that both the CDs before and after doping have O–H stretching vibration peaks at 3430 cm−1 [40,41]. The C–H stretching vibration causes the weak peaks at 2800-3000 cm−1 [42]. The peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 1520 cm−1 are due to CO and CC stretching peak respectively [43,44]. The characteristic peak at 1390 cm−1 exhibits COO− and the peak at 1168 cm−1 is due to phenolic C–O stretching vibrations [45,46]. Compared to the undoped CDs, the doped CDs have new peaks at 3190/2065/1665/138/618 cm−1 that can be respectively attributed to N–H/-SCN or NCS/CN/C–N/C–S groups, which indicates the successful doping of N, S elements [47–50]. Additionally, the surface functional groups of CDs become more abundant and complex after doping revealed by the FT-IR pattern, with varying energy levels. This abundance and complexity may result in a series of surface defects, namely emission potential wells, leading to a red-shifted emission [40,51].

To better investigate the cause of the doping-induced ultra-wide redshift of CDs, we further analyzed the surface elements and chemical states by XPS technique and compared them with the undoped CDs. Fig. 2 a illustrates that the XPS full spectrum of doped CDs exhibits five distinct peaks positioned at the binding energies of 285 eV (C 1s), 532 eV (O 1s), 400 eV (N 1s), 227 eV (S 2s), and 163 eV (S 2p), respectively [45]. This indicates that they contain the above four elements with the corresponding contents of 53.55%, 10.53%, 29.15%, and 6.77%, respectively (Fig. 2f). Compared with them, the undoped CDs do not have the presence of N, and S characteristic peaks and contain only two elements, C and O (Fig. 2a), with the corresponding contents of 78.8% and 21.22% (Fig. 2f). The high-resolution XPS (HR-XPS) spectrum provides information about the bonding arrangements and distinctions between CDs doped and CDs without doping. Fig. 2b displays the HR-XPS spectrum of C 1s, which can be fitted into four sub-peaks positioned at 284.2 eV (C–C/CC), 285.7 eV (C–O/C–N/C–S), 288.2 eV (CO/CN/CS), and 289.0 eV (-COOH) [17,50]. It is easy to see that undoped CDs lack N and S elements, thus their high-resolution C 1s spectrum does not exhibit relevant chemical bonds. Whereas the doped CDs show more groups bonded to C, probably due to the involvement of more C from the precursor in the synthesis of the CDs, which also induces an increase in the C/O ratio. Furthermore, Fig. 2c displays the high-definition XPS spectrum of O 1s, exhibiting two distinct peaks at 531.6 eV (CO) and 532.4 eV (C–OH/C–O–C) as a result of split-peak fitting [52]. The relative rise in the proportion of CO peaks after doping can be partially attributed to the increased –COOH content (Fig. 2b), indicating enhanced oxidation on the surface of CDs [50]. Additionally, Fig. 2d and 2e presents the XPS spectrum of N 1s and S 2p for CDs with doping. The detailed spectrum of N 1s can be separated into three peaks: C = N/pyridine N (398.4 eV), pyrrole N (400.2 eV), and graphite N (400.6 eV) [17,53]. It is evident that pyrrole N and graphite N are the leading contributors, which could potentially explain the redshift in emission caused by doping. Furthermore, –C = N indicates the existence of the surface moiety –SCN. The detailed spectrum of S 2p can be separated into the C–S bonds of S 2p3/2 (161.8 eV) and S 2p1/2 (163.0 eV) [28]. These results are consistent with FT-IR data, revealing the N, S co-doped CDs contain multiple functional groups and form polyaromatic structures with a high degree of surface oxidation.

The optical properties of CDs were examined by analyzing the UV–vis absorption and FL spectrum in methanol solution, both before and after doping. The absorption peaks of the doped and undoped CDs are depicted in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S3. These peaks are most prominent at approximately 245 nm, with an extra peak at 290 nm. In the aromatic sp2 structural domain, the presence of the π-π* transition of the CC bond is responsible for these peaks, and this effect is more pronounced in the undoped CDs [54,55]. The peak at 328 nm proves the existence of the n-π* transition of the CO bond. Furthermore, the undoped CDs display a faint wide absorption between 340 and 450 nm, whereas the doped CDs reveal a fresh wide peak around 528 nm (450–600 nm). This observation suggests that the existence of N, S-related surface-rich functional groups, generates new surface states that expand the absorption spectrum of CDs [56].

The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence (PL) spectrum indicate that the co-doped CDs exhibit a maximum emission peak of 575 nm at the optimal
λ
ex of 528 nm. In Fig. 3b, PL decay curves at the optimal excitation are displayed, and the calculated FL lifetime of co-doped CDs is 8.21 ns (refer to SI for detailed calculations) [56]. Additionally, Fig. 3c and 3d exhibit the PL spectrum of doped CDs at various
λ
ex and concentrations (
λ
ex = 395 nm), respectively. The co-doped CDs show essentially excitation-independent emission of 575 nm (Fig. 3c) with increasing excitation wavelength, which is caused by a uniform and well-yielding CD surface [40]. Notably, at lower excitation wavelengths (380–450 nm), the PL spectrum generates a new peak (400–500 nm), exhibiting double-peaked emission (Fig. S4a). The intensity of this peak gradually diminishes and its position gradually shifts towards the red until reaching 450 nm as the
λ
ex increases. The reason for this could be that various types of functional groups generate a range of energy levels on the CDs' surface. These energy levels, in turn, combine with multiple functional groups to create “surface states”, causing the formation of different emission centers [39,56]. Consequently, the primary emission centers vary with different excitation wavelengths, resulting in slightly different colors for the doped CDs under various excitations (Fig. S4b). The findings of the PL spectrum depicted in Fig. 3d indicate that the peak position remains largely unaffected, aligning with the excitation independence of CDs. From Fig. 3e and 3f (see Fig. S4c for a localized enlarged view), the luminescence intensity of the CDs with N, S co-doping remains essentially unchanged with extremely small decay even after a two-week placement (λex = 395 nm), demonstrating excellent optical stabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent mechanism of the wide PL redshift
ce:section.ce:para: Researchers have proposed various PL mechanisms, such as size effects (carbon nucleation states), surface states, molecular states, and cross-linked enhanced emission effects [7]. In this work, TEM, HR-TEM, Raman spectroscopy, and XRD patterns show that the doped CDs have lower graphitization levels with sp2 structural domains, and more surface defects with disordered sp3 structures; therefore, the PL ultra-wide redshift after doping may be related to their surface defect states and disordered structures. The FT-IR and XPS spectrums further prove this conjecture. The co-doped N and S introduce more abundant functional groups (CN, –SCN/NCS, etc.) on the surface of CDs, as well as nitrogen in different chemical forms (C–NC, C–NH–C, and C3–N) can form a series of surface energy traps between the energy gaps of CDs to form different energy levels, i.e., mid-gap states. Consequently, the band gap is effectively narrowed, leading to a red-shifted FL emission. Furthermore, the N, S co-doped CDs exhibit an increased –COOH concentration and oxidation level on their surface. The bonding of sp2 hybridized carbon to carboxyl groups induces noticeable local deformation, resulting in reductions of the band gap between LUMO and HOMO and causing a shift towards a longer FL emission peak [57]. Additionally, the absorption spectrum, along with the PLE spectrum, indicates that the PL is influenced by three excitation regions. The excitation bond near 328 nm (320–340 nm) can result from the n-π* transition of the CO bond, while the band near 400 nm (340–450 nm) ought to be linked to the transition of the emitting states related to O. Furthermore, the band within the vicinity of 528 nm (450–550 nm) should be linked to the transition of the emitting states related to N and S. The PL spectrum of the emission with two peaks correspondingly indicates distinct processes of electron transition.

Using these findings and deductions, we suggest potential structural patterns and energy states to account for the extensive redshift of FL from undoped CDs to N, S co-doped CDs. The cooperative interaction of the surface oxidation states and the defective levels is responsible for the ultra-wide redshift observed in Fig. 4 a. After being exposed to radiation, the electrons in HOMO can be stimulated to LUMO, as seen in Fig. 4b. Afterward, for undoped CDs in the absence of thiourea dopant, few electrons in the excited states are jumped to the states related to O. Consequently, the recombination of these O-related state electrons with holes in HOMO leads to the emission of blue PL (
λ
ex = 400 nm,
λ
em = 445 nm). By N, S co-doping with using thiourea, the O-related states experience alterations. Specifically, there is an increase in the –COOH content, surface oxidation, and a further reduction in the band gap. Additionally, most electrons in the excited states are jumped to the states related to N and S, resulting in an ultra-wide PL redshift from blue to orange (
λ
ex = 528 nm,
λ
em = 575 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The solvent engineering and preparation of solvent-controlled multicolor CDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to Gao et al. [50], the increase of –COOH content and surface oxidation may be caused by solvent engineering, so the solvent environment in the solvothermal process was changed to further investigate and confirm the importance of solvent on the redshift of CDs emission. As seen in Fig. S5a, solvent-controlled multicolor CDs (M-CDs) were prepared, and Fig. S5b shows that the λem of blue/green/yellow/orange CDs (B-/G-/Y-/O-CDs) at 395 nm excitation wavelength were located at 450/515/541/573 nm, respectively, showing blue, green, yellow, and orange luminescence. The PLE spectrum shows that the λexmax of M-CDs are 411/496/505/528 nm, respectively (Fig. S6). The corresponding PL spectrum at the optimal excitation wavelengths is also given. Fig. S7 shows the PL spectrum of M-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. G-/Y-/O-CDs have good excitation independence, except for B-CDs. In addition, Y- and O-CDs synthesized by chloroform and ethanol as solvents exhibit a double-peak emission phenomenon similar to that of doped CDs synthesized by methanol at various λex, which also corresponds to the multiple excitation bands in their PLE spectrum.

It was obvious that the CDs after doping synthesized with ethanol and methanol as solvents had similar PLE spectrum, optimal excitation wavelength, and PL emission peak position. We hypothesized that the solvent engineering was related to the ultra-wide red shift between doped CDs and undoped CDs. To test this, undoped samples were also synthesized corresponding to solvent-controlled M-CDs and compared the optical photographs (λex = 395 nm), PL emission (λex = 395 nm, λex max) of the diluted samples before and after doping as shown in Fig. S8 (see Fig. 1 for methanol samples). The results confirmed our hypothesis: the emission peak positions of the undoped samples under ethanol and methanol as solvents are the same (the absorption spectra are also essentially the same, Fig. S8g). In other words, the reaction system under ethanol and methanol environment achieved the same PL ultra-wide redshift after the introduction of a single dopant thiourea, while other solvents did not cause significant emission peak redshift. Based on the high agreement between ethanol and methanol solvents, we measured the PL spectrum of the doped CDs prepared with alcoholic solvents as well as some other solvents. As shown in Fig. S9, there is a tendency to show double-peak emission in alcohol solvents with decreasing polarity and blue-shifted peaks; this shows a positive solvent-degenerate phenomenon. In contrast, other solvents show a negative solvent-degenerate phenomenon of blue-shifted PL with increasing solvent polarity; the doped CDs prepared in these solvents exhibit blue or green PL emission.

The mechanism of how N, S doping, and solvent engineering work together to induce the ultra-wide redshift of PL emission is still unclear. Previous studies have attributed similar phenomena to the H-bonding and polarity of the protonic solvent and the CDs, but this does not explain why N, S co-doped CDs under water engineering do not show long wavelength emission. Moreover, most studies have focused on solvent-dependent CDs, which are dispersed in solvents without reacting with them, rather than solvent-controlled CDs, which are involved in the solvothermal reaction [58,59]. The role of solvent engineering in affecting the formation process of CDs in conjunction with doping has not been well investigated and remains a great challenge. According to certain reports, the solvent effect is mainly realized by the solvent attaching to the surface of the CDs or forming different emission potential wells on their surfaces [60]. In our work, the high consistency of methanol and ethanol solvents in terms of PL shifts before and after doping may result from their interaction with different surface states of CDs. We propose that the alcoholic environment, especially the more polar methanol and ethanol solvents, may also influence the surface oxidation and defect states of CDs during the solvothermal process before and after doping with thiourea. In other words, like N, S co-doping, methanol and ethanol solvents act as an indispensable role in PL emission ultra-wide red-shift, but their specific pathways and processes need further exploration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The solvatochromism and water responsiveness of the N, S co-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Unlike solvent-controlled CDs, solvent-dependent CDs exhibit a solvatochromism that can be simply attributed to hydrogen bonding between the solvent and the CDs or its polarity [58,59]. The doped CDs synthesized by methanol as the solvent control are uniformly dispersed in different solvents (DMSO, DMF, methanol, ethanol, formamide, water, ethylene glycol, acetone, chloroform, toluene, and cyclohexane) (Fig. S10a and S10b ), because they are rich in polar functional groups (-COOH, –OH, etc.) on surface and show certain FL changes under UV excitation, indicating their good dispersion and solvent dependence.

The emission wavelength of doped CDs did not change clearly when dispersed in water, but the FL intensity decreased (Figs. S10c and S10d). Based on this FL change in methanol-water mixtures, we prepared a probe for the detection of water content therein. Fig. S11a shows that the FL of doped CDs was high in methanol (λex = 395 nm), and decreased with increasing water volume fraction. The PL spectrum of doped CDs under 395 nm UV excitation confirmed this trend when the water volume fraction changed from 0% to 100% (Fig. S11b). The data analysis showed that the FL intensity changes I0/I of the mixed solution had an exponential function (1) with the water volume fraction VWater (%) of the mixed solvent when it was in the range of 0–90% (Fig. S11c), and I0 means FL intensity of the solution without water.
(1)
I 0
I
= 0.621
exp
− 50.282
V water
− 118.3
,
R 2
= 0.998
In addition, as can be seen from Fig. S10, when CDs were dispersed in certain solvents, their peak positions and intensities did not differ much from those dispersed in methanol, so we added an additional 0.5 mL of the impurity solvent to the above-mixed solvent with a water content of 20%, as a means of verifying the anti-interference ability of the fabricated CDs-based water detection probe. As can be seen from Figs. S11d and S11e, when the impurity solvent is present, the measured peak position of the probe is basically unchanged, and the intensity ratio I/I0 changes very little, which shows a good anti-interference ability.

The feasibility of the method for the determination of water in organic solvents was evaluated using methanol as the experimental sample with different water contents. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries of water in methanol ranged from 99.2% to 101.6%. The results indicated that the method can accurately recover the spiked water in methanol and is expected to be used for the determination of water in organic solvents.

However, when the water content in the mixed solvent varied from 0% to 10%, the FL intensity change was very small. Therefore, the water content varying from 0% to 10% or even 0%–1% may be undetected, i.e., the doped CDs have limited sensitivity in detecting water content in mixed solvents, and they can only detect higher water content.

CDs can recognize specific metal ions with the help of functional groups on their surface, such as Ag+, Fe3+, Zr4+ [11]. To achieve high selectivity and sensitivity detection performance, we used the doped CDs to detect different metal ions and various concentrations. To avoid the interference of other factors on the sensitivity of detecting Mn2+, we measured the PL spectrum of doped CDs of different pH and NaCl concentrations in aqueous solutions (Fig. S12). The relative PL intensities (I0 represents the PL intensities of aqueous solutions without NaCl) and the emission peak positions of CDs were almost constant at different NaCl concentrations, indicating good stability (Figs. S12a and S12b). Similarly, when the pH changed from 3 to 9 (the same range as the pH of aqueous solutions of various metal ions), the PL intensity and peak position were almost unaffected (Figs. S12c and S12d). The decrease in FL intensity at very low or high pH possibly results from the protonation and deprotonation of –COOH; while a small blue shift of the peak position probably indicated that the extremely acidic or basic environment of CDs may influence their molecular states [61].

The selectivity of CDs for various metal ions was first tested by recording the FL intensity changes after adding CDs to aqueous solutions of several common metal ions (concentration at 500 μM, pH = 7) and mixing for 10 min to stabilize the FL intensity. Fig. 5 a and Fig. S13a show the PL spectrum of CDs mixed with different ionic solutions of 395 nm excitation wavelength and the corresponding histograms for the intensity relative ratios I/I0. The FL intensity of CDs mixed with Mn2+ solutions decreased the most. Fig. S13b shows that the FL of doped CDs was significantly quenched by Mn2+ under UV excitation. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. S14, when other metal ions are present, the quenching effect of Mn2+ on the FL of CDs is almost unaffected, which indicates that CDs are highly selective for Mn2+. These findings indicated that CDs with N, S co-doping can selectively detect Mn2+. For comparison purposes, we attempted to identify Mn2+ using CDs that were not doped (Fig. S15). Surprisingly, the undoped CDs retained their initial blue FL, and the intensity of FL remained relatively unchanged even after the introduction of Mn2+. The large number of functional groups, especially N, S-related groups, attached to the surface of the CDs with co-doping suggests that they may be responsible for the excellent selectivity of the doped CDs for Mn2+ detection. Additionally, the numerous groups introduced through S doping can readily bind with metal ions [62]. Consequently, Mn2+ can swiftly bind to these groups of the CDs with N, S co-doping, potentially leading to the formation of non-luminescent compounds.

To further examine the impact of Mn2+, we analyzed the FL burst of doped CDs at varying concentrations of Mn2+. The PL spectrum corresponding to these concentrations is illustrated in Fig. 5b. The high sensitivity of doped CDs for Mn2+ is indicated. The PL intensity of the mixed solution containing Mn2+ decreased as the concentration of Mn2+ increased from 0 to 500 μM (Refer to Fig. S16 for the corresponding optical photographs). Furthermore, a strong correlation exists between the concentration of Mn2+ [Mn2+] from 0 to 40
μ
M and the intensity ratio I0/I (Fig. 5c). This relationship can be accurately represented by equation (2).
(2)
I 0
I
= 0.00589
Mn
2 +
+ 0.998 ,
R 2
= 0.996

The equation I0/I = KSV [Mn2+] + 1 is comparable to the Stern-Volmer equation, where KSV denotes the Stern-Volmer constant; I0 and I represent the FL intensity with or without the inclusion of Mn2+, respectively. Under the same experimental conditions, equation (3) can be used to estimate the limit of detection (LOD) for Mn2+.
(3)
LOD =
3
S d
K SV
Where Sd denotes the standard deviation of the I0 for 10 parallel measurements without Mn2+. It is calculated that the LOD of Mn2+ is 0.13 μM. The feasibility of the method for the determination of Mn2+ in water was assessed by adding MnCl2 to tap water to prepare samples with different Mn2+ concentrations. The recoveries ranged from 99.9% to 101.0% with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 1% as shown in Table S3. The results indicated that the method can accurately detect Mn2+ in real samples and is expected to be practically applied to the determination of Mn2+ in water.

It is worth mentioning that the color change of the doped CDs aqueous solutions at daylight, as depicted in Fig. S17. It can shift from a pale red to blue when the [Mn2+] increases, which provides a qualitative estimation of the detection of Mn2+. Flocculated state solids were visible at the bottom of the bottle, which may indicate their potential applicability in water treatment [63]. Consequently, the CDs with N, S co-doping have the potential to serve as highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probes for detecting Mn2+. Table S1 provides a compilation of CDs-based fluorescent probes that have been utilized for the detection of Mn2+ in recent years. The CDs-based fluorescent probes employed in this study exhibit extended wavelengths without requiring supplementary material composites or modifications, while simultaneously showcasing exceptional Mn2+ detection capabilities.

Next, we explored the reaction mechanism of CDs interacting with Mn2+. The current FL quenching mechanism is mainly composed of two ways, dynamic quenching and static quenching. Testing the change in FL lifetime is the most precise method to differentiate between these two mechanisms [63]. Fig. 5d demonstrates that the CDs' FL lifetime diminished from 8.21 ns to 6.18 ns following interaction with Mn2+, indicating the involvement of a dynamic quenching mechanism [64]. As seen in Fig. 5e, the UV absorption spectrum proves that the strongest peak at 245 nm undergoes some blue shift, as well as the weakening of the shoulder peak at 290 nm, which may suggest that the quenching process involves complex reactions and the formation of new compounds. The absence of the peaks around 328 nm and 340–600 nm suggest that the n-π* transition attributed to the CO bond is suppressed and its surface state undergoes complex changes, indicating that a static quenching process is involved [64,65]. Meanwhile, the zeta potential (determined by the average of three measurements) changed from −2.70 mV to +13.16 mV after the addition of Mn2+ (Fig. 5f and Fig. S18) for N, S co-doped CDs, indicating that they could be bound to the surface of CDs due to electrostatic adsorption, and contact with various functional groups on their surfaces to form new non-luminescent compounds as well as violent molecular collisions, triggering both static and dynamic quenching processes, further proving the above conclusion [66,67]. The FL quenching process of Mn2+ on CDs is an outcome of the cooperative interaction of static and dynamic quenching effects.

In conclusion, a simple and effective strategy for the preparation of CDs with long wavelengths available for the detection of Mn2+ was designed. By adding thiourea as a single dopant and introducing nitrogen and sulfur at the same time, it formed more abundant groups on the surface of the CDs and thus modulated their defect energy levels and surface states. We not only successfully shifted the position of the emission peaks of the CDs to a longer wavelength (∼130 nm), but also achieved a highly selective and sensitive detection of Mn2+. The LOD was 0.13 μM, and the linear range of detection was 0–40 μM. These results reveal a feasible method for the preparation of CDs with long-wavelength emission and provide insights into how to simultaneously construct CDs-based fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions without involving other complex compounding or modification processes. In addition, it is expected to be practically applied in the future for the detection of metal ions for which there are no effective analytical means or the analytical process is too cumbersome and complex.
